content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA consisting of 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N integers.

You can perform the following operation any number of times.


Choose any iii (1≤i≤2⋅N)(1 \le i \le 2 \cdot N)(1≤i≤2⋅N) such that AiA_iAi​ is even and divide AiA_iAi​ by 222.
Or choose any iii (1≤i≤2⋅N)(1 \le i \le 2 \cdot N)(1≤i≤2⋅N) and multiply AiA_iAi​ by 222.
Find the minimum number of operations required to make the number of even elements and number of odd elements in the array equal.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the array AAA is 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N.
The second line of each test case contains 2⋅N2 \cdot N2⋅N space-separated integers A1,A2,…,A2⋅NA_1, A_2, \dots, A_{2 \cdot N}A1​,A2​,…,A2⋅N​ denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of operations required to make the number of even elements and number of odd elements in the array equal.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N (the array size is 2·N). The second line contains 2·N space‑separated integers A1 … A2·N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the minimum number of operations required to make the number of even elements equal to the number of odd elements.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: No operations are required since there are already an equal number of odd elements and even elements.
Test case 2: One operation is enough. It can be seen that the number of odd and even elements in the resulting array are equal.
[1,3,4,8,2‾,6]→÷2i=5[1,3,4,8,1,6][1, 3, 4, 8, \underline{2}, 6] \xrightarrow[\div 2]{i = 5} [1, 3, 4, 8, 1, 6][1,3,4,8,2​,6]i=5÷2​[1,3,4,8,1,6]
Test case 3: Here two operations are enough to make the number of odd and even elements in the resulting array equal.
1st operation: [1,5,3,5‾,6,7]→×2i=4[1,5,3,10,6,7][ 1, 5, 3,\underline{5}, 6, 7] \xrightarrow[\times 2 ]{i = 4} [1, 5, 3, 10, 6, 7][1,5,3,5​,6,7]i=4×2​[1,5,3,10,6,7]
2nd operation: [1,5,3,10,6,7‾]→×2i=6[1,5,3,10,6,14][ 1, 5, 3, 10, 6, \underline{7}] \xrightarrow[\times 2 ]{i = 6} [1, 5, 3, 10, 6, 14][1,5,3,10,6,7​]i=6×2​[1,5,3,10,6,14]

solution.approach: Let E be the current number of even elements. If E equals N, no operation is needed. If E < N we have too many odds; turning any odd into an even costs exactly one operation (multiply by 2), so the answer is N‑E. If E > N we have too many evens; turning an even into an odd requires dividing by 2 repeatedly until the number becomes odd. The number of required divisions for a value x is the exponent of 2 in its factorisation, v2(x). For each even element compute v2(x), sort these values increasingly and sum the smallest (E‑N) of them – this yields the minimal total operations. The algorithm runs in O(2N log 2N) per test case (sorting), which is fast enough because the total number of elements over all test cases is ≤ 2·10^5.
solution.timeComplexity: O(totalElements · log totalElements) – sorting the v2 values of evens in each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(totalElements) for storing the v2 values of evens.
